title: Logical inference and consistency

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Knowledge Representation and Reasoning plus Reasoning Under Uncertainty; atomic topic: Logical inference and consistency

canonicalExplanation.summary: Inference applies sound rule steps to premises: forward chaining derives reachable consequences, backward reasoning reduces a query to supporting subgoals, and results can be entailed, contradicted, or unknown.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: From bird(tweety) and bird(x) -> animal(x), forward chaining derives animal(tweety) with a recorded two-premise proof path. Your goal is to trace an inference chain and distinguish entailed, contradicted and unknown statements. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: AI problems and agents; Problem and environment characteristics; Symbols, predicates and rules. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Inference applies sound rule steps to premises: forward chaining derives reachable consequences, backward reasoning reduces a query to supporting subgoals, and results can be entailed, contradicted, or unknown. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Soundness differs from completeness, cyclic rules need termination control, inconsistent premises may derive both sides, absence is not contradiction, and a proof is relative to the encoded rules. The learner must reject this overclaim: A conclusion that cannot be proved is contradicted, and adding more rules can never create inconsistency.

includes: Apply forward or backward rule reasoning and identify contradictions or unsupported conclusions.
Trace an inference chain and distinguish entailed, contradicted and unknown statements.
excludes: Soundness differs from completeness, cyclic rules need termination control, inconsistent premises may derive both sides, absence is not contradiction, and a proof is relative to the encoded rules.

outcomes: Trace an inference chain and distinguish entailed, contradicted and unknown statements.

walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: From bird(tweety) and bird(x) -> animal(x), forward chaining derives animal(tweety) with a recorded two-premise proof path.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Track proof support and classify entailed, contradicted, both, or unknown according to the logic and consistency policy; lack of proof alone is not negation.
